sn47.7:1.1: “There are in the Himalayas, the king of mountains, difficult, uneven areas where neither monkeys nor human beings wander.
sn47.7:1.2: There are difficult, uneven areas where monkeys wander, but not human beings.
sn47.7:1.3: There are level stretches of land, delightful, where both monkeys and human beings wander.
sn47.7:1.4: In such spots hunters set a tar trap in the monkeys’ tracks, in order to catch some monkeys.
sn47.7:2.1: Those monkeys who are not foolish or careless by nature, when they see the tar trap, will keep their distance.
sn47.7:2.2: But any monkey who is foolish & careless by nature comes up to the tar trap and grabs it with its paw, which then gets stuck there.
sn47.7:2.4: ‘I’ll free my paw,’ he grabs it with his other paw.
sn47.7:2.6: Thinking, ‘I’ll free both of my paws,’ he grabs it with his foot.
sn47.7:2.8: Thinking, ‘I’ll free both of my paws and my foot,’ he grabs it with his other foot.
sn47.7:2.10: Thinking, ‘I’ll free both of my paws and my feet as well,’ he grabs it with his mouth.
sn47.7:2.12: So the monkey, snared in five ways, lies there whimpering, having fallen on misfortune, fallen on ruin, a prey to whatever the hunter wants to do with him.
sn47.7:2.13: Then the hunter, without releasing the monkey, skewers him right there, picks him up, and goes off as he likes.
sn47.7:2.14: “This is what happens to anyone who wanders into what is not his proper range and is the territory of others.
sn47.7:3.1: “For this reason, you should not wander into what is not your proper range and is the territory of others.
sn47.7:3.2: In one who wanders into what is not his proper range and is the territory of others, Māra gains an opening, Māra gains a foothold.
sn47.7:3.3: And what, for a monk, is not his proper range and is the territory of others?
sn47.7:3.7: Forms cognizable by the eye—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked to sensual desire.
sn47.7:3.11: Tactile sensations cognizable by the body—agreeable, pleasing, charming, endearing, enticing, linked to sensual desire.
sn47.7:3.12: These, for a monk, are not his proper range and are the territory of others.
sn47.7:4.1: “Wander, monks, in what is your proper range, your own ancestral territory.
sn47.7:4.2: In one who wanders in what is his proper range, his own ancestral territory, Māra gains no opening, Māra gains no foothold.
sn47.7:4.3: And what, for a monk, is his proper range, his own ancestral territory?
sn47.7:4.7: There is the case where a monk remains focused on the body in & of itself—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.7:4.8: He remains focused on feelings in & of themselves…
sn47.7:4.10: mental qualities in & of themselves—ardent, alert, & mindful—subduing greed & distress with reference to the world.
sn47.7:4.11: This, for a monk, is his proper range, his own ancestral territory.”
